text: The hysteroscope is introduced through the adequately dilated cervical os and advanced to the uterine fundus. Bilateral osteo are visualized, with no septum or other abnormalities observed in the uterine cavity.
The hysteroscope is retracted to the lower uterine segment, revealing a concave defect at the anterior wall, which visually indicates the niche and thinning of the anterior uterine wall integrity.

The surgeon uses a hysteroscopic resectoscope to denude the concave defect in the lower uterine segment, removing tissue from the cavity. Following this, the hysteroscope is removed and a uterine manipulator is inserted into the uterine cavity.
The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, visually confirming the uterine niche as evidenced by the presence of a uteroperitoneal fistula tract and dense adhesions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon carefully removes dense omental adhesions using cold laparoscopic scissors, taking care to avoid the bladder during the dissection.
The surgeon begins dissection laterally near the obliterated umbilical vessels on each side to enter the vesicovaginal space.

The surgeon uses a CO-II laser to make an incision on the peritoneum, creating new spaces on both the right and left sides. The dissection is gradually extended through to the vesicocervical space, combining the newly created bilateral spaces.

The surgeon utilizes the CO-II laser alongside blunt dissection to mobilize the bladder, demonstrating precise and superficial dissection that minimizes thermal spread and reduces the risk of injury to the bladder.
The surgeon carefully dissects inferiorly, creating adequate space for the upcoming excision and repair of the niche, ensuring precision to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects dense fibrotic tissue layer by layer, identifying and mapping out the fistula tract until reaching the defect at the anterior uterine wall.

The surgeon removes the uterine manipulator and introduces the hysteroscope transvaginally to the lower uterine segment, advancing into the niche under direct laparoscopic visualization. The thinned myometrium is visible due to the extrusion of the hysteroscope and illumination, along with pressure from the hysteroscopic saline infusion, facilitating laparoscopic mapping and excision of the defect.

The surgeon performs a wide laparoscopic excision of the peripheral tissue surrounding the niche, gradually resecting it until the hysteroscope penetrates into the abdominal cavity. The excised fistula tissue is then sent for pathological examination.

The surgeon repairs the defect in two layers using zero V-lock suture. Fresh bleeding at the edges is controlled with desiccation, achieving good hemostasis. The edges are re-approximated, and the repair is confirmed to be intact.
